speaker: interviewer
text: January 29, 1997. Interview with Max Veffer, V-E-F-F-E-R. Interviewer, Lisa Newman in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. The interview is in English. January 29, 1997. Interview with Max Veffer, V-E-F-F-E-R. Interviewer is Lisa Newman, Toronto, Ontario, Canada, and the interview is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, my name is Max Veffer. And I was born in Amsterdam, which is in Holland. And I was born May 18, 1928. And the place, if I can remember, was Waterloo Square, which was, I think, was next to a Portuguese synagogue. My story starts there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the names of your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom's name was Sara, and her maiden name was Nebig, Sara Nebig. And my dad's name was Jonas. And he was a very kind man. I loved him very dearly. And then I had an older brother. His name was Jack. And I had a sister-- I mean, I still have her-- Chellie. And then there is myself, Max, my brother, Alan. He passed away. May he rest in peace. And there was my brother, Joe, and my kid brother, Johnny. And they were all born in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you know about the history of your family? Did they live in Holland for many generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My grandfather, great-grandfather, and as far as I know, they were all in Amsterdam. They were all born in Amsterdam. That's as much as I can remember there. I lived, maybe, for four years in Amsterdam. And then we moved to a small town, Bussum. And Bussum is like a beautiful little town. It's like big homes, and people very friendly, like a small town, like small town people are. And we took-- they took right to my father because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your family move?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because my dad used to peddle flowers in-- in Bussum. And he used to stand in the square, in the middle of town, near the city hall. And he used to sell flowers. And eventually, he wanted to open up a flower shop, which he did. So that's why we moved to Bussum. And the flower shop was right near the rail station, railway station, and I remember that very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: The address was, I believe, was 30 [DUTCH]. [DUTCH] is like Dutch, because we put the numbers before. We put them after. And I remember very well from the day that we moved in, actually, because I think I was about five years old and I used to go to kindergarten. I remember that far back. Also, I remember going to first grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I remember about that? Because of all the kids, I was the one that had a bike, a small bike. My dad won a bike in the lottery. We had lotteries. They have lotteries here. They had lotteries in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he bought me a bike. And it was very nice. I remember the first grade because I remember the teacher. At a young age-- I don't know how it is possible-- but I had a crush on my first grade teacher. She was a nice lady. That's the reason I remember her, probably. Because I surround myself-- even today, I surround myself with nice people. Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Fiverberg. ?] See, it came to me. As of today, I try to remember yesterday, but today I remember her name. So she was nice lady. I remember that. I remember second grade, also a lady, Mrs. [? Bell. ?] See, that came to me too. And third grade, I believe it was a man by the name of [? Pleuchard. ?] He was a Frenchman, but he moved from France to Holland. And he used to tell stories, oh, beautiful stories. And I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. I went to Heder. I went on Sunday. I went to Heder. And I forgot the first rabbi's name, but the second one I remember vividly because he was with us right through the war. And his name was Rabbi [? Fangeldun. ?] Nice man, very nice man. And I remember having my bar mitzvah. I do remember that too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason I remember that, because that was almost the last time that the whole family was together. That's the reason I remember that. My big present was a pen and pencil set. I remember that too. And they told me after-- I wish they had video then-- they told me I had a real beautiful voice. I [INAUDIBLE] real good. So my dad told me I did a very good job. And I was happy with that--

words.text: And I was happy with that--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened on that day? Can you remember from the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the family was there, and we didn't throw parties like we throw today. And when our family came together, it was anywhere from 50 to 100 people. So they all came back to the house. We had a house full of people. And then we had a party at the house because of Maxie's bar mitzvah. But my name was not Maxie. In those days, my name was Mey-Pie. Everybody was with an I-E. Even my name, my birth name, was Meyer. But nobody called me Meyer. The only person that called me Meyer was my gym teacher. He was the only one that called me Meyer. But ever since then, nobody ever called me Meyer. They called me Mey-Pie. And I was known as Mey-Pie until I went to-- until after the war, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I enjoyed school because we always went with three children. Why I say three children? We had actually six, but the first one that went to school was my brother Jack. Then my sister Chellie followed, and me. So we always had company going to school. And I remember vividly, it was a very close knit together family.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I come out of school, [INAUDIBLE] talking about things that you remember. I was a fighter. Although I was very small, I was a fighter. I come out of school, and somebody is beating up on my brother. No, well that can't happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go over there. I beat him up. But I got beat myself. I got a cut on my eye. I came home. My mother said, what'd you do? Said, well somebody beat up on Johnny. Said, oh, that's good. She said, did you get hurt? I said, no, not really. So while we have supper, the bell rings. And, and my dad says, hey, you have to come downstairs. You beat somebody up? No, I don't remember. I'm already afraid. I mean, my father never laid a hand on me. He was strong, but he never laid a hand on his-- so we come downstairs. There's a policeman, and the guy is about a foot taller than me. And the policeman says, did you beat him up? Says, yeah. I says-- he says to the other fellow, he says, yeah, it'd be a shame, that a little guy like that beat you, beat this guy? Get out of here. And they got out of here. And it's one of those stories-- I got a few of these stories. You told me a story about apples?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. That's right. On Wednesday afternoons-- I had three friends, and we always would go out, have a good time on a Wednesday afternoon. This afternoon, we go picking apples. But they were not ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to go to the farm. There's a lot of farmland there. So we arrived there. You have to first climb a fence, and then you have to go to-- it's like where they keep manure. So we had to go along a ledge.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we go along the ledge, and we're picking apples. As soon as I'm up the tree, and my buddy yells there, the farmer's coming with his dogs. I'm getting out of there. And as I go along the ledge, [INAUDIBLE], I go into the manure. And I smelled pretty good. So it was good thing it was summertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's-- around where we live, there's a pond. So you know what, I says to my friends, we better wash out our clothes. So we wash out the clothes, and we lay out in the sun until the clothes are dried. Excuse me. We come home, and my mother says, Mey-Pie, you smell from something. What did you do? I said, oh, I didn't do nothing, ma. She said, yeah, you smell from something, and it doesn't smell good. You better tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't lie. I said, yeah, well, we went to pick apples, and I fell into the manure. She says, good for you. Serves you right. You should have fallen [? headfirst. ?] You should have go put your head into the manure. I said, no that I didn't do. So she washed off our clothes. And that was the end of that story. But the other story that didn't go so good, was-- I was playing-- I'm a soccer player from when I was 6 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a very good soccer player, you tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I don't want to sound conceited, but I was good. I was pretty good at that. Because I liked it. I'll tell you a story during the war, what happened. I'm getting ahead of myself. We play soccer, and [INAUDIBLE] dad, but I didn't have nothing to do. I usually have something to do after school. I go on my bike and deliver some parcels for him. And he gives me a nickel. A nickel in those days was good. It's better than getting $1 today. Because with a nickel, you can do a lot of things in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the afternoon, I have nothing to do. I go take my ball. There's-- the taxis are standing near the station. And the guys always played, played with me because, I don't know, that was a usual thing. And there's a ball, gets kicked up on top of the roof. How am I going to get this thing off, I says? They weren't going to go up there. This little guy gets himself up on the roof. Said, oh, guys, I got the ball. And I looked down there. Who's standing down there? My dad. I said, oh my god. And he's yelling at the top of his voice. He says, you better get down here. I'll break your neck. Be careful! You know, he's standing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you come down. I said, yeah, yeah, yeah. If you go away, I'll get down there. No sooner her left, I get down there. I don't know how I'm going to get into the store because he's standing there waiting for me like, like, you know, he's going to kick me, you know. But I know he wasn't going to kick me. And I figured I better--

speaker: interviewer
text: And home, home was above the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, home was above. I have to get through the store [INAUDIBLE]. He says get in. I said, yeah, sure. I'll wait till you get a customer. Then I'll go in. So sure enough, he gets a customer, and I flip in there and I get upstairs because I know I won't get the supper. I did something bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm upstairs in my pajama, and I'm hungry. But I know my mother is going to come up. Sure enough, knock on the door. Says, here's your food, but you better not tell your dad that I gave you food. You know, and those were the things, you know. I had a lot of fun-- mischief, but nothing bad. Nothing really bad. Tell me about how you lived up above the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: Above the store, we had, well, there was like the living room, I remember. And then we had a dining room. And then there was a third floor. I slept with my three brothers, and my oldest brother had a room by himself. And my sister had a room by herself. It was quite big, quite big, yeah. And we lived there very nice. Yeah, we had a good life there. The life before the war was much nicer than it is today. I liked the days of the horse and buggies. It was much nicer than today. I remember the horse and buggy because I used to help a fellow out loading up potatoes. And the nicest thing that they let me do, he let me ride the horse. I loved it. I loved it. I used to help him out, and I ride the horse. And that was-- like I said, Bussum reminds me of the outskirts of Toronto-- all farmland, beautiful. Maybe you could walk, walk for miles. And yes, it was very, very nice. We had-- my dad always provided for us good. We were always well dressed, well fed. And that's maybe why we got through the war so well, because my mother was a very good cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did she cook for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the usual things. A Dutch meal is a nice plate of soup-- soup is good for you-- potatoes, vegetables. [INAUDIBLE]. Holland had a lot of, lot of potatoes, vegetables, cheese, you know. So that was the main course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat much meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. Chickens. She cooked a lot of chickens. Fish. We were big fish eaters because they used to fish from the North Sea, you know, the catfish from the North Sea. So Holland is a small country, but they provided a lot of food for themselves. They export a lot, even still today they are, do. So they [INAUDIBLE], but mostly it was home grown stuff. And he used to come, a fellow to the door with his card, that would sell potatoes, all the vegetables stuff. And the milkman used to come to the house. You know, like You saw So the same type of a guy that would come with his little horse, or he pulled himself. And my mother bought whatever milk she needed. And it would come every day or every other day. And that was-- this was a very, very simple life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother bake for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mostly. She did her own baking and her own cooking. And Sundays, my dad and my mom would go to the soccer game. That was a favorite pastime on Sunday. We used to love it. The reason he took me because I was the only one that played soccer. And I was pretty active as a kid. I used to go as a gymnast on Tuesday night, I used to go. And on Sunday my dad would take me. And the biggest thing was for me when he take me to the big games in Amsterdam, like Holland against Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in those days, I was about eight, nine years old, and my father never lied. But he lied about my age. Why did he [INAUDIBLE] my age? Children five years and younger go for nothing. But I was already eight, nine years old. And we would come to the gate. They said, how old is this guy? Five years. He'll sit on my knee. And he would take me in, wouldn't pay for me. And that's the only time he would lie, a little white lie. Everybody uses a white lie once in a while. So that's how he got me into the game.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very [? close ?] as a child because he took me everywhere, you know. He loved his other children too, but like I said, you love your children all differently. But because I took so much interest in sports and he liked sports, so that's why they took me all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You helped him a lot in the store as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, like I said, I come home, and I had my things to do. The Dutchmen were very clean. The first chores was outside, the sidewalk. Take the hose, wash out the-- you know, wash the sidewalk. Then I used to help clean up the garbage, clean the flowers. And if there was a couple of deliveries to be made, then that's how I made my little pocket money. My biggest money he ever paid me was a quarter. I tell you, that was-- that was something. You get a quarter, you can go to a show with that. Matinees cost only a quarter, so that's where we used to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that when you ate upstairs, you all had to be silent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We didn't talk. Reason was, we ate, and we couldn't hear the bell. Because maybe he stayed open until about 7:00, 8:00. And during suppertime, everybody quiet. Nobody spoke. When you're finished and off the table, my dad was downstairs. We'd go back to-- but we were usually, we were in bed at 7:00. Up to, I remember, up to the age of 11, 12, 7:00, everybody upstairs. And that's the way we were brought up, very strict. Maybe we benefited from it, as today, we benefit by the way we were brought up. And like I said, there were no favors. Yeah, I think my dad favored my sister. I always thought that. And you know, well, she was a girl. It was the only girl between the four guys, you know, or the five guys. So I'm sure they favored her. And like I said, my mother always thought the way you dress your children, that's the way people treat you. If they look well, they treat you well. And it's a true thing. And my favorite pastime was, in those days, go to Amsterdam to see my family. And I didn't wait for nobody. I was eight, nine years old. I put myself on the train and go. And then I knew my father and mother would, they were going to come in the afternoon. But I didn't care. I go by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would you go to visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had a cousin. His name was also Jonas, and he was like a brother to me. We used to do everything. I'd go there and get off the train. And there was a-- I knew which bus to take to get to where they were. It was not a bus, streetcar. And I knew it-- number nine. See, I remember number nine. It was number nine, and it takes you right in front, into the Jewish ghetto. And there were a lot of Jewish people in Amsterdam, a lot of them, a lot of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from that Jewish part of Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have a word here. In Holland, we used slang. We didn't-- we speak Dutch, not Jewish. I learned the Jewish here. But a word like hamish, that's the word to use. It was hamish. You walked in a Jewish, in the Jewish district, and you knew you were in between Jewish people. You were in-- like they say, the deyn eygn those words-- I never knew those words, but now I know. It's your deyn eygn. And in between your deyn eygn, you're comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your own people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, your own people. And you know you were in between your own people. And I used to go to the market. They had like-- at Waterloo Square, where I was born, they used to have stalls. They sell vegetables. It's like something similar like you have a flea market. But it wasn't a flea market because it was regular prices. My uncle Louie, he was married to a sister of my dad. And they had a stall. OK. And I used to go there, and eat [INAUDIBLE]. I couldn't eat the fruit as much as they [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] They couldn't to give me enough. And I used to stay over with them sometimes because I used to help them count the money. See, I remember these things. It's funny how you remember these things. I used to help him count the money, because dimes and nickels and quarters and whatever. I used to help him pile them up on the table and count the money, how much he took in. Says, OK, you did a good job. And he gave me a dime. I made a dime helping him. This was good. He was a very nice man. He was almost like a father to me. Because he used to take me to the market. Especially, I used to go days that I knew he was going to the market. I get up early in the morning, get myself to Amsterdam, and he takes me to the big market. And that was an out for me. And we go there, and he buys merchandise. And then we go back and help him put the stuff out, the apples. I had myself a good time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit your grandparents in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Glad you remind me. I used to go to school, and lunch time. So my grandfather had a wholesale vegetable fruit market-- not a market, like it's like a wholesale. And it was just about five minutes from the school. And she knew me already. My little brothers and sisters never go. My sister never go. I used to go. I liked my grandmother. She was nice lady. That was on my mother's side. I used to have my lunch there. My grandfather was a very strong, strong person, but nice. And I remember when he passed away. I was old enough to remember he was very sick. I don't know what he passed away from, but he passed away when I was a child. And then he had his one, two, three sons. There was-- if I remember the names correctly, it was Judah, one of the names was Judah, one name was Morris, and it was Abbi Abram. She had all the names, biblical names-- Abram, Judah, Joseph. But we never called them [INAUDIBLE]. It was [? Api, Yopi, ?] you know. Those were typical Dutch names. I used to go with him, Morris. Morris took me all the time to the market as long as I got there early enough. Because he used to go at 5:00, 6:00 in the morning. And I get myself up.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother says, why do you want to get up so early? I said, I'm going with Uncle Morris. I'm going to the market. I used to like that. I was a busybody. Yeah, keep me busy. And today, still busy. I'm supposed to be retired, but I'm not. What do you remember about the relationships between Jews and non-Jews before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was pretty good. It was pretty good. They took us in. I mean, a matter of fact-- I went to-- the school that we went through, I mean, we were-- maybe there was about 20 Jewish kids going to that school. My friends were basically Gentile. But they were good kids. Like I-- for some reason, I always surrounded myself with people that I like. If I don't like you, forget it. But I'm surrounded by people that I like so they could like me back. OK. And I had this particular friend-- and if I can remember-- his name was Anton, Tony. Here, they would say Tony. And he would fight my battles because there were still some people that didn't like, disliked the Jewish people, but not too many. At one time, I remember somebody called me dirty Jew. And he-- I didn't do the fighting. He did the fighting for me, which I thought was very nice. And I remember that as today. But as far as people disliking us, no, there wasn't too much of that. Because I remember my dad-- he was a member of the soccer team, and he was the president of the social club. And he used to put on the show. And they loved him. My father was a likeable person anyways, but they loved him for doing that. Once a year we used to have a get together, you know, and he made the-- he was the social club director. And no, I don't think so. There was not that that much. After the war, it was totally different story. That's the reason we left Holland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come to that. Was there music in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Music. I always liked to play an instrument, but I didn't have no patience to learn. But my sister played the accordion. Instead of me, she learned. Yes, it was nice. We liked music. And my brother Jack played the guitar. When we had parties, I'd get up on the table and start singing. I don't care what it was. Sometimes I didn't know the words, but I sang anyways. Because they told me that I had a pretty good voice. And yes, we had parties. We used to get together at least-- I would say, once a month, the whole family get together at our house, or we would all-- we go to Amsterdam, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the whole family, how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh. On my father's side, he had three sisters and two brothers. And on my mom's side, they also had four brothers and three sisters. A matter of fact, a sister of my mother married a brother of my father. And yes, I would say an average be about 30 to 40, 50 people when we get together. Yeah, and we had a real good time. Always have a good time, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you listen to the radio at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radio, radio, yes, we did listen to the radio. Because that's the only thing there was. There was no TV. I remember going to-- I used to go to a gym class on Tuesday night, and I would run home because my favorite program was on. They let me stay up a little later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What program was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god. There was two comedians, Snip and Snap, I think they called them. And I loved them. They were very funny. And the show was on for either a half hour or hour. I can't remember exactly. But after the show, I had to go to sleep. But yes, there was-- I remember that. There was a lot of fun. It was a lot of fun before the war. That's the reason why I remember pleasant days. There was nothing bad happened. Really, I can't remember anything bad happening before the war, not really. Unless I don't remember, but I do not. It was a very pleasant life before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family keep Shabbat at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: We not-- were not a very religious family. The reason for that was, my dad had to keep the store open on Saturday. So God so forgive us, that's how he made his living. So we didn't really keep a kosher house. But we did kept Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW] or something to that effect. I remember these little things. But I remember my dad, to his best of his knowledge, or my grandfather when he was still alive, his Hebrew was pretty good. And he used to conduct a Seder. Yes, I remember that. Yom Kippur, we would not keep open. Or the Jewish holiday, we would not keep open.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too often. Not too often because, like again I said, I don't want to sound hypocritical-- we believe in God. We do believe in God. We know we're Jewish. But in our own way, we were Jewish. My dad, like, again, we-- actually because he had the store open. And he kept a very liberal home as far as being Jewish. And that's maybe the reason we didn't go. But I did go to Heder. He did make us go to Heder. And my brother went to Heder. My younger brothers were too young to go, but by the time their age was, we were in hiding. You know, so therefore, they didn't go. I remember that from those days. And there's something that [? sits ?] me, but I can't remember at the moment. But the worst thing that was, I remember-- the bad thing I can remember-- 1939 because that's the first thing that when the bad things started happening to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we get to that, could I just ask, did you ever travel out of Holland before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no. The only thing-- see, it's a good thing you remember. From one thing comes another. What my mom did do, we used to go to the North Sea every summer. My dad got an apartment for her, for us. And every summer, we used to go like near the North Sea. Big beach, long beach, nice sand, and we used to enjoy it every summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, basically, like a holiday. It was basically like a big, big holiday for us. We used to look forward to that, to go to Zandvoort. It's called Zandvoort. I couldn't think of the name for a minute. Zandvoort, yes, nice. That was very nice. I remember that. And my grandfather and my grandmother used to come down. And, you know, the whole family actually because on my father's side, his two sisters used to take their kids there. It was like a family outing for the summertime. We used to be there from July till we go back to school in September. Yeah, I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a short break.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's good.
